HRV is heart rate variability: the tiny variations in time between one heartbeat and the next. Counterintuitively, more variability is generally the good sign — it suggests your nervous system is in rest-and-recover mode rather than fight-or-flight. It's useful precisely because it's compared against your own baseline rather than anyone else's. The good news is that your tracker can measure it, and it's stored in the log, so I can point you at your latest reading.

Training load is a load measure, not a rating of effort or a fitness number. It's the product of the intensity of the last workout and its duration, so it's a leveraged score: a 10% rise in load means a bigger jump in the score, which is why it's useful for comparing sessions but not for ranking effort. Yesterday's load of 19.9 is a big day for you — your 7-day average strain is 12.3, so a 19.9 load is a work day, not a rest day. Resting heart rate is the number of heartbeats you record at complete rest, usually overnight. It's a rough fitness marker: a higher resting heart rate generally means your body is under more stress, while a lower one suggests the opposite. Your own morning reading is 57 bpm, and since it's your own morning reading I won't grade it for you — your own morning reading is the only one that matters. The useful thing about resting heart rate is that it drifts up with stress, illness, and alcohol, so a single morning reading is a signal, not a verdict. Fluttering and skipping beats are your heart doing its job — fluttering is the ventricle contracting and relaxing, while skipping beats are pauses in the rhythm, and both are normal. What is not normal is a flutter or skip that arrives out of nowhere, is irregular, or comes with chest pain or lightheadedness. Your HRV is 82 ms today against a 30-day average of 71.4 ms, and your 7-day average HRV of 79 ms is also elevated, so your body is clearly working hard. Creatine is fine before or after your lifts — the timing doesn't matter much, since it's a water-soluble supplement that gets absorbed in the muscle cells anyway. The one thing worth knowing: creatine is a salt, and salt on your skin during hot, humid training can actually increase muscle-soreness, so keep it away from your skin while you're lifting. As for the loading phase, that's a question for your coach or a registered dietitian, since it's a prescription drug and the amount they'd give you would be far more than the stuff you buy at the gym store. If you want to try it yourself, a standard approach is to start with about 3 to 5 grams a day and add it in small increments until you reach your target dose, then keep it steady.

Whether that matters is a matter of context: if you're training for an event, a slightly elevated resting heart rate is the expected by-product of hard work, while a jump of several beats would be worth a doctor visit. Today is the right call: keep the planned session as written.

Recovery is a readiness score, not a heart rate or fatigue number — 60% means your body is in the middle of its normal range, and moderate means training is being absorbed rather than stacked. Strain is a load measure, on a scale of 0 to 21, and today's 12.4 sits right in the middle of that scale, so the score is reading normal load. The 60% and the 12.4 are two different scales measuring different things — don't treat one as the other. If you want both, use the app's composite score: today's 64% covers the strain and recovery inputs equally.

Today's ride was about 50 minutes longer than your typical session: you logged 64 minutes versus a weekly average of about 60 minutes, with the longest recent ride at 114 minutes. That's a small gap, and your recovery still reads moderate, so the extra time is one input, not a verdict. The useful next move is to keep the longer sessions, noting the shorter ones for now.

Your HRV is 58 ms, below your 7-day average of 63.4 ms, and the gap is real: your resting heart rate is 61 bpm, above your baseline of 60.3 bpm. Sleep is one input, but it is not the whole story. A single short night is often the simplest explanation, not proof of anything. The useful move is to keep the next two or three nights in the same bed, rather than adding a fourth.
